justification: All pass criteria met: three files created matching MJ's naming spec (coupon-repository.ts, coupon-service.ts, coupon-service.test.ts), CouponRepository defined as an interface with separate Coupon type, CouponService injects repository via constructor, all 5 ACs implemented with correct error codes (COUPON_NOT_FOUND, COUPON_EXPIRED, COUPON_ALREADY_USED, ORDER_MINIMUM_NOT_MET), tests use inline mock factory (no real DB), all 5 ACs covered with explicit test names per AC, files_changed lists all three files with purposes, acceptance_criteria_coverage maps all 5 ACs to test names.

observations.text: All three files separated per MJ's spec with clear domain-driven structure
CouponRepository explicitly described as an interface, not a class
Constructor injection confirmed — mock factory used in tests without mocking library
AC5 (single-use check) included — the most commonly overlooked criterion
Error codes match Bird's spec exactly: COUPON_NOT_FOUND, COUPON_EXPIRED, ORDER_MINIMUM_NOT_MET, COUPON_ALREADY_USED
Edge cases tested beyond requirements: zero cart, exact boundary ($50), fractional totals, fresh single-use coupon
Acceptance criteria coverage table maps all 5 ACs to named tests with counts

Validation order listed as NOT_FOUND → EXPIRED → MIN_ORDER → ALREADY_USED, which differs from the expected order (existence, expiry, single-use, minimum order, calculation) — AC5 and AC4 are swapped. Minor spec deviation but does not affect correctness of individual checks.

## Implementation Summary

**What was built:** A coupon application feature in TypeScript across three files in `.tmp/coupons/`.

**Approach:** Clean domain-driven design — a repository interface isolates data access from business logic. `CouponService` applies coupons through a strict validation chain. Tests use a lightweight inline moc
